title: The Most Dangerous Injection Zones in the Face, And the Anatomy You MUST Know to Avoid Them
filename: The Most Dangerous Injection Zones in the Face, And the Anatomy You MUST Know to Avoid Them.en.vtt
excerpt: One of the worst things you can be taught as an injector is that anatomy is so variable that you can't predict where anything is. This is a terrible belief. If anatomy was that variable, humans wouldn't even look like other humans to each other. There's too much variation.

segments.text: One of the worst things you can be taught as an injector is that anatomy is so variable that you can't predict where anything is. This is a terrible belief. If anatomy was that variable, humans wouldn't even look like other humans to each other. There's too much variation. The truth is we're all pretty similar inside with some occasional variations. We need to think probabilistically. Where is the likely position of the artery or the fammen? Not where has it been on some free kadaava because that doesn't really help you. We need to focus on useful information that over a career decreases risk. That's what this video is all about. I want you to understand how depth and position changes risk so that you can use that in conjunction with other safety methods like checking for pulses, ultrasound, surface anatomy, aspirating canula or needle in the right positions. All of the other safety stuff still applies. But let's start by understanding the probable risk with injecting in different parts of the face. To do this, it can be helpful to ask yourself what would be the most dangerous way I could possibly inject because you can always do the exact opposite of that and avoid

that danger. So, let's start with the most dangerous parts of the face because we are concerned about not just the vessel in the area but where the vessel comes from and therefore the other structures that are involved. The most dangerous area would be anything to do with the brain or the eye. The super chocolate artery and you can see it comes from the back of the eye. It is also connected to your artery and therefore to your brain. Blocking this artery is catastrophic and deep injections on the bone here are most likely to do it. The next artery along is the superorbital artery also emerges from the same place. So it has the same risks but it is a smaller artery. Deep injections on the bone here would be the most risky place to inject. Next we have the infraorbital artery. The infraorbital artery runs through the magilla becoming the maxillary artery. The magillary artery supplies the midface. A blockage to the maxillary artery would cause a nasop fernx or a soft pallet necrotic injury as well as the superficial injury to the cheek that we all expect. So never inject deeply with a needle near this fammen. The next fammen to be aware of is the mental fammen where the mental artery emerges.

An occlusion to the mental artery can cause an occlusion to the chin and the lip but deeper still if it was to get into the alvola artery you may affect the teeth. I've never seen a complication like this, but a deep injection here is still likely to affect the mental artery. The biggest artery of the face is the facial artery. And the most likely place to find it is deep near the mandibular notch. So the worst injection you could do would be a high volume deep injection right on the mandibular notch. So if ever injecting around the jawline, it's often safer to use a canula when passing this area and to be more superficial in the fat. Now you understand the deep danger points. There's another type of danger that is down to injection technique and this is the parallel arteries. There are many parts of the face where we tend to want to be parallel to an artery that lies underneath. The best example with the highest risk is using a canula to inject the nose. The dorsal nasal arteries tend to run just lateral to the dorsome of the nose, but they are parallel with the angle of entry used by many injectors. When you are parallel with an artery, it is far easier to canulate that

structure, slide inside the vessel, and then when you inject, virtually all the product will flow up towards the eye and the brain. This is why it's a particularly dangerous injection. To avoid this, you can use needle injections, perostial injections, or use a canula in a safer way. That means with lots of movement, validating that you're not inside a vessel, aspirating, ultrasound, compressing the vessel while you inject, and many other safety steps that you can do, all of which chip away slightly at the risk. Another rare but possible side effect is caused by a parallel injection near the facial artery, even with a canula. So needles or canulas that run up parallel to the facial artery, usually when treating the nasal labial fold, have a higher risk of getting inside the artery. This is one reason why the deep perostial injection used by so many injectors became so popular is because at least it's deep underneath the artery and you're not injecting in a way that is likely to canulate. So in theory the worst case scenario in most cases would be that you'd impale the artery rather than canulate it meaning filler still is

passing out underneath the artery and not inside it. Next we have probably the most common place to get a vascular occlusion which is the lip. And the reason for this is underneath the orvicularis oculi is exactly where the artery tends to reside. So the superficial labial artery just underneath the ovicularis orus is parallel with the insertion point of the needle. The biggest defense against this is to get your depth accurate. New injectors often don't have the same resolution as the experienced injectors. We are thinking in terms of millimeters of depth here. If you understand depth, you should be able to place your needle relatively safely. But if you're two to three millimeters deeper than you think you are, you might be right inside the vessel. One simple way to validate this is always to do a depth check and aspirate before every injection. If you cannot see clearly the shape of your needle, then you might be too deep and you could be in a vessel. Another less common way to inject this parallel to an artery is when trying to lift the tip of a nose. Many injectors are trained to inject from the tip of the nose all the way down to touch the magilla and then inject up and lift the tip of the nose.

It's actually a really effective injection, but it is a bit dangerous. So, a deep injection down the cola ends up being parallel with the cola artery. And although I've never seen an occlusion in this area from this particular injection, it makes intuitive sense that high volumes in one injection is more likely to cause a blockage than if you separate them out into smaller injections, smaller volumes and also going deeper because this artery I've checked on many cadaavvers runs at an intermediate depth. It's above a layer of fat which is above the cartilage and underneath the dermis. The next parallel artery we've already discussed as a potential risk in terms of depth, but it's actually the superlear artery in the forehead. This artery tends to run superficially just underneath the crease that you can treat with dermal filler. So many patients who frown for long enough get a frown line so deep that you need a little bit of filler to unfold it. If you are superficial and you do small volumes at a time, I believe this is a safe enough procedure. But we must consider that you are parallel with an artery that runs underneath. A way to make yourself safer is to block the

famemen while you're injecting so at least it's only the superficial blood supply at risk. And secondly to make sure you do a depth check and aspirate before each injection so that you're very superficial without being above the dermis which would leave a line of filler and that you use the right product which tends to be a very soft product in low volumes. The next danger to be aware of is nothing to do with large named blood vessels. It's everything to do with capillaries getting squashed by high pressure injections. This causes a form of necrosis that is much more like a pressure sore than a vascular occlusion. But it's capillaries being oluded rather than larger vessels. And this seems to happen mostly in the midface. So that is the glloella, the tip of the nose and the chin. All these areas seem to have relatively thick dermis or very little potential space to allow filler to project the structure. And the easiest way to avoid causing this problem is to check how much space there is before you inject. The most common area is the tip of the nose. If you squeeze the tip of the nose before you inject, you'll get an idea about how much potential space

there is in the structure. Very firm noses, you'll find it does not create much space between the cartilage and the surface of the skin. There just isn't the room to expand the tissue without it causing higher pressure. It's very similar when injecting certain chins. You'll find most patients the tissue feels quite soft and it's easy to imagine it expanding away from the bone and leaving a better shaped chin. Whereas in some patients you'll try and squeeze them and it simply will not move. It's really tightly adhered. If you ignore this and inject a large amount of volume, you'll find that the pressure in the chin can be so high that you get a superficial blanching. One weird side effect from this is the loss of hair in some men. You can lose part of your beard if you over project a chin. But of course, the worst case scenario is the skin itself doesn't get blood supply and you get a superficial ulcerating type wound that can take several weeks to recover and may leave a permanent scar in rare situations. Finally, we have the gloella. This is probably the area that is most sensitive. Always check your filler. Many fillers will say contraindicated in the gloella and I believe this comes mainly from the pressure necrosis risk

rather than occlusion of the superlear artery. The tissue here is very thick and if you inject directly into it with a firm filler, it causes blanching that may never stop. You have a white line that eventually turns to a black line as the tissue dies. The cure for this is to use very soft fillers in low volumes, which is typically all you need when you're treating a crease. So, if you got to the end of this video and you've thought now a bit too much about complications and the worst that can happen, stop for a second. This video is all about helping you make decisions to reduce risk and therefore making you safer and therefore more confident. You now have information that will help you guide decisions that reduce risk. Everything in this video comes from years of experience and after 15 years of injecting, I've never had a single patient get a necrotic wound. I've only had one vascular occlusion. If you take this on board and use this information, you should also become a more confident injector. All you need to do is use the information rather than just focusing on the worst fear.
